Carry moments with Hensel-selected square root glued by CRT

Let $p=29$.

Define
$$
n=1+p^6+p^{15}+p^{23}+p^{31},\qquad k=p+p^{11}+p^{17}+p^{24}.
$$

Write the base-$p$ expansions
$$
k=\sum_{i\ge 0} a_i p^i,\qquad n-k=\sum_{i\ge 0} b_i p^i,
$$
with $0\le a_i,b_i<p$. Define carries by $c_{-1}=0$ and, for each $i\ge 0$,
$$
a_i+b_i+c_{i-1}=d_i+p\,c_i,
$$
for a unique digit $d_i$ with $0\le d_i<p$.

Define the carry invariants
$$
C=\sum_{i\ge 0} c_i,\qquad W=\sum_{i\ge 0} i\,c_i,\qquad Q=\sum_{i\ge 0} i^2\,c_i,
$$
and let
$$
V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right).
$$

Define
$$
A\equiv p^{W}+5C\pmod{125}.
Let $y$ be the unique integer with $0\le y<125$ such that
y^2\equiv A\pmod{125}\quad\text{and}\quad y\equiv C\pmod{2}.
Let $X$ be the unique integer with $0\le X<1000$ satisfying
X\equiv y\pmod{125},\qquad X\equiv Q\pmod{8}.
$$

Finally, let $N$ be the unique integer with $0\le N<1000$ satisfying
$$
N\equiv (p^4+3)C+(p^2+7)W+(p+11)V+(p^3+5)Q+X+n\pmod{1000}.
$$

Determine $N$.

Solution.
Step 1 (sparse_base_p_representation): Identify the nonzero base-$p$ digits of $n$ and $k$.
From $n=1+p^6+p^{15}+p^{23}+p^{31}$, the base-$p$ digits of $n$ satisfy
- $n_0=n_6=n_{15}=n_{23}=n_{31}=1$,
- $n_i=0$ for all other $i\ge 0$.
From $k=p+p^{11}+p^{17}+p^{24}$, the base-$p$ digits of $k$ satisfy
- $a_1=a_{11}=a_{17}=a_{24}=1$,
- $a_i=0$ for all other $i\ge 0$.

Step 2 (borrow_chain_decomposition): Compute the base-$p$ digits of $n-k$.

Lemma 1 (block expansion): For integers $s<t$ and base $p\ge 2$,
$$
p^t-p^s=\sum_{j=s}^{t-1}(p-1)p^j.
$$
Proof: $p^t-p^s=p^s(p^{t-s}-1)=p^s(p-1)(1+p+\cdots+p^{t-s-1})$. Distributing gives exactly $\sum_{j=s}^{t-1}(p-1)p^j$. Each coefficient is $p-1<p$, so this is already a valid base-$p$ digit block.

Write
$$
n-k=1+(p^6-p)+(p^{15}-p^{11})+(p^{23}-p^{17})+(p^{31}-p^{24}).
Apply Lemma 1 to each difference:
p^6-p=\sum_{j=1}^{5}(p-1)p^j,
$$
$$
p^{15}-p^{11}=\sum_{j=11}^{14}(p-1)p^j,
$$
$$
p^{23}-p^{17}=\sum_{j=17}^{22}(p-1)p^j,
$$
$$
p^{31}-p^{24}=\sum_{j=24}^{30}(p-1)p^j.
$$

Therefore the base-$p$ digits of $n-k$ are:
- $(n-k)_0=1$,
- $(n-k)_i=p-1$ for $i\in\{1,2,3,4,5,\ 11,12,13,14,\ 17,18,19,20,21,22,\ 24,25,26,27,28,29,30\}$,
- $(n-k)_i=0$ for all other $i\ge 0$.

Step 3 (carry_position_interval_analysis): Determine the carry pattern in the base-$p$ addition $k+(n-k)=n$.

Lemma 2 (forced carry chain on a full $(p-1)$ block): Fix a base $p\ge 2$ and suppose that for some integers $s\le t$:
- $a_s=1$ and $a_{s+1}=\cdots=a_t=0$,
- $b_s=b_{s+1}=\cdots=b_t=p-1$,
- the incoming carry is $c_{s-1}=0$,
- and additionally $a_{t+1}=b_{t+1}=0$.
Then $c_s=c_{s+1}=\cdots=c_t=1$ and $c_{t+1}=0$.
Proof: At $i=s$, $a_s+b_s+c_{s-1}=1+(p-1)+0=p$, so $c_s=1$. For $s<i\le t$, we have $a_i=0$, $b_i=p-1$, incoming carry $1$, so $a_i+b_i+c_{i-1}=0+(p-1)+1=p$, hence $c_i=1$. At $i=t+1$, the sum is $0+0+1=1<p$, so $c_{t+1}=0$.

We apply Lemma 2 to each $(p-1)$ digit block of $n-k$ from Step 2:

- Block $1$ to $5$: Here $a_1=1$, $a_2=\cdots=a_5=0$, and $b_1=\cdots=b_5=p-1$. At digit $0$ we have $a_0=0$, $b_0=1$, so $c_0=0$ and therefore the incoming carry into digit $1$ is $c_0=0$. Also $a_6=b_6=0$ (since $k$ has no $p^6$ term and $n-k$ has digit $0$ at $6$). Hence Lemma 2 gives $c_1=\cdots=c_5=1$ and $c_6=0$.

- Block $11$ to $14$: Here $a_{11}=1$, $a_{12}=\cdots=a_{14}=0$, and $b_{11}=\cdots=b_{14}=p-1$. Since digits $7,8,9,10$ of both addends are $0$ and we already have $c_6=0$, no carry can appear in digits $7$ through $10$, so $c_{10}=0$ and the incoming carry into digit $11$ is $0$. Also $a_{15}=b_{15}=0$. Lemma 2 gives $c_{11}=\cdots=c_{14}=1$ and $c_{15}=0$.

- Block $17$ to $22$: Here $a_{17}=1$, $a_{18}=\cdots=a_{22}=0$, and $b_{17}=\cdots=b_{22}=p-1$. Between digits $15$ and $16$ both addends have digit $0$ and we already have $c_{15}=0$, so $c_{16}=0$. Also $a_{23}=b_{23}=0$. Lemma 2 gives $c_{17}=\cdots=c_{22}=1$ and $c_{23}=0$.

- Block $24$ to $30$: Here $a_{24}=1$, $a_{25}=\cdots=a_{30}=0$, and $b_{24}=\cdots=b_{30}=p-1$. Since $c_{23}=0$, the incoming carry into digit $24$ is $0$. Also $a_{31}=b_{31}=0$. Lemma 2 gives $c_{24}=\cdots=c_{30}=1$ and $c_{31}=0$.

All other $c_i$ are $0$. Hence the carry indices are exactly
$$
\{1,2,3,4,5\}\cup\{11,12,13,14\}\cup\{17,18,19,20,21,22\}\cup\{24,25,26,27,28,29,30\}.
$$

Step 4 (carry_moment_summation): Compute $C=\sum c_i$.
There are $5+4+6+7=22$ carry positions, so $C=22$.

Step 5 (carry_moment_summation): Compute $W=\sum i c_i$.
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} i=15,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=11}^{14} i=\frac{(11+14)\cdot 4}{2}=50,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=17}^{22} i=\frac{(17+22)\cdot 6}{2}=117,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=24}^{30} i=\frac{(24+30)\cdot 7}{2}=189.
$$
Thus $W=15+50+117+189=371$.

Step 6 (carry_moment_summation): Compute $Q=\sum i^2 c_i$.
$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} i^2=55,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=11}^{14} i^2=121+144+169+196=630,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=17}^{22} i^2=289+324+361+400+441+484=2299,
$$
$$
\sum_{i=24}^{30} i^2=576+625+676+729+784+841+900=5131.
$$
Therefore $Q=55+630+2299+5131=8115$.

Step 7 (kummer_carry_valuation_bridge): Compute $V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$.

Lemma 3 (Kummer's theorem): For a prime $p$ and integers $0\le k\le n$, $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries in the base-$p$ addition of $k$ and $n-k$.
Here $p=29$ is prime and $0<k<n$, so the theorem applies.

The number of carries is $C=22$, hence $V=22$.

Step 8 (multiplicative_order_via_LTE): Determine $\operatorname{ord}_{125}(29)$ and reduce $29^{W}$ modulo $125$.

Lemma 4 (LTE order lifting for $5^k$ when $v_5(a-1)=1$): Let $p=5$ and let $A$ be an integer with $5\mid (A-1)$ and $5\nmid A$. Then for every integer $t\ge 0$,
$$
v_5(A^{5^t}-1)=v_5(A-1)+t.
$$
Proof: This is the standard Lifting-the-Exponent lemma (LTE) in the case of an odd prime $5$ applied to $A^{5^t}-1$, since $5\mid (A-1)$ and $5\nmid A$.

Apply Lemma 4 with $A=29^2$. We have
$$
29^2-1=840=5\cdot 168,
$$
so $v_5(29^2-1)=1$, and $5\nmid 29^2$. Therefore, for $t\ge 0$,
$$
v_5\!\left(29^{2\cdot 5^t}-1\right)=1+t.
$$
In particular:
- for $t=2$, $v_5(29^{50}-1)=3$, so $29^{50}\equiv 1\pmod{125}$;
- for $t=1$, $v_5(29^{10}-1)=2$, so $29^{10}\not\equiv 1\pmod{125}$.

We now compute $29^{25}\pmod{125}$ explicitly to rule out order $25$:
First compute
$$
29^2\equiv 841\equiv 91\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^4\equiv 91^2=8281\equiv 31\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^5\equiv 31\cdot 29=899\equiv 24\pmod{125}.
$$
Then
$$
29^{25}=(29^5)^5\equiv 24^5\pmod{125}.
$$
Compute
$$
24^2=576\equiv 76\pmod{125},
$$
$$
24^4\equiv 76^2=5776\equiv 26\pmod{125},
$$
$$
24^5\equiv 26\cdot 24=624\equiv 124\equiv -1\pmod{125}.
$$
So $29^{25}\equiv -1\pmod{125}$, hence $29^{25}\not\equiv 1\pmod{125}$.

We have shown $29^{50}\equiv 1\pmod{125}$ but $29^{25}\not\equiv 1\pmod{125}$, so
$$
\operatorname{ord}_{125}(29)=50.
$$

Step 9 (modular_exponentiation_reduction): Compute $p^{W}\pmod{125}$.
Since $\operatorname{ord}_{125}(29)=50$, we have $29^{W}\equiv 29^{W\bmod 50}\pmod{125}$. Because $W=371$ and $371\equiv 21\pmod{50}$,
$$
29^{371}\equiv 29^{21}\pmod{125}.
Using the intermediate values from Step 8,
29^{10}\equiv (29^5)^2\equiv 24^2\equiv 76\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^{20}\equiv 76^2\equiv 26\pmod{125},
$$
so
$$
29^{21}\equiv 29^{20}\cdot 29\equiv 26\cdot 29=754\equiv 4\pmod{125}.
$$
Thus $p^{W}=29^{371}\equiv 4\pmod{125}$.

Step 10 (modular_reduction_mod_125): Compute $A$.
With $p^{W}\equiv 4\pmod{125}$ and $C=22$,
$$
A\equiv p^{W}+5C\equiv 4+5\cdot 22=114\pmod{125}.
$$

Step 11 (quadratic_residue_check + hensel_lifting_square_root_mod_5_power): Compute $y$.
We solve $y^2\equiv 114\pmod{125}$ and then enforce $y\equiv C\pmod{2}$.

First reduce modulo $5$:
$$
114\equiv 4\pmod{5}.
$$
The congruence $y^2\equiv 4\pmod{5}$ has exactly two solutions:
$$
y\equiv 2\pmod{5}\quad\text{or}\quad y\equiv 3\pmod{5}.
$$

Lemma 5 (Hensel lifting for square roots, simple-root case): Let $f(x)=x^2-A$ and $p=5$. If $x_0^2\equiv A\pmod{5^r}$ and $5\nmid 2x_0$, then there exists a unique $x_1\pmod{5^{r+1}}$ with $x_1\equiv x_0\pmod{5^r}$ and $x_1^2\equiv A\pmod{5^{r+1}}$.
Proof: Write $x_1=x_0+5^r t$. Then
$$
x_1^2=x_0^2+2x_0\cdot 5^r t+(5^r t)^2.
$$
For $r\ge 1$, $(5^r t)^2$ is divisible by $5^{2r}\supseteq 5^{r+1}$, so modulo $5^{r+1}$ we need only solve
$$
x_0^2+2x_0\cdot 5^r t\equiv A\pmod{5^{r+1}}.
$$
Dividing by $5^r$ gives a linear congruence for $t$ modulo $5$, and $2x_0$ is invertible modulo $5$ by hypothesis, so $t$ (hence $x_1$) is unique.

We lift each root from modulo $5$ to modulo $25$ and then to modulo $125$.

(1) Lift the root $y\equiv 2\pmod{5}$.
To modulo $25$, write $y=2+5t$:
$$
(2+5t)^2=4+20t+25t^2\equiv 4+20t\pmod{25}.
$$
We need $4+20t\equiv 114\equiv 14\pmod{25}$, so $20t\equiv 10\pmod{25}$. Dividing by $5$ gives $4t\equiv 2\pmod{5}$, hence $t\equiv 3\pmod{5}$ and we may take $t=3$, giving $y\equiv 17\pmod{25}$.
To modulo $125$, write $y=17+25s$:
$$
(17+25s)^2\equiv 17^2+2\cdot 17\cdot 25s\pmod{125}.
$$
Compute $17^2=289\equiv 39\pmod{125}$ and $2\cdot 17\cdot 25=850\equiv 100\pmod{125}$. Thus
$$
39+100s\equiv 114\pmod{125}
$$
so $100s\equiv 75\pmod{125}$. Dividing by $25$ gives $4s\equiv 3\pmod{5}$, hence $s\equiv 2\pmod{5}$ and we take $s=2$, giving $y\equiv 67\pmod{125}$.

(2) Lift the root $y\equiv 3\pmod{5}$.
To modulo $25$, write $y=3+5t$:
$$
(3+5t)^2=9+30t+25t^2\equiv 9+5t\pmod{25}.
$$
We need $9+5t\equiv 14\pmod{25}$, so $5t\equiv 5\pmod{25}$ and $t\equiv 1\pmod{5}$; take $t=1$, giving $y\equiv 8\pmod{25}$.
To modulo $125$, write $y=8+25s$:
$$
(8+25s)^2\equiv 8^2+2\cdot 8\cdot 25s\pmod{125}.
$$
Compute $8^2=64$ and $2\cdot 8\cdot 25=400\equiv 25\pmod{125}$, so
$$
64+25s\equiv 114\pmod{125}
$$
hence $25s\equiv 50\pmod{125}$, so $s\equiv 2\pmod{5}$; take $s=2$, giving $y\equiv 58\pmod{125}$.

Therefore the two solutions modulo $125$ are $58$ and $67$.

Now enforce $y\equiv C\pmod{2}$. Since $C=22$ is even, we need $y$ even, so the unique admissible choice is
$$
y=58.
$$

Step 12 (CRT_mod_1000): Compute $X$.
We have $X\equiv y\pmod{125}$ with $y=58$, and $X\equiv Q\pmod{8}$.
Compute $Q\bmod 8$: since $8115=8\cdot 1014+3$, we have $Q\equiv 3\pmod{8}$.
So
$$
X\equiv 58\pmod{125},\qquad X\equiv 3\pmod{8}.
$$
Write $X=58+125t$. Modulo $8$, this gives
$$
X\equiv 58+125t\equiv 2+5t\pmod{8}.
$$
We need $2+5t\equiv 3\pmod{8}$, so $5t\equiv 1\pmod{8}$. Since $5^{-1}\equiv 5\pmod{8}$, this gives $t\equiv 5\pmod{8}$. Taking $t=5$ yields
$$
X=58+125\cdot 5=683,
$$
which already lies in $0\le X<1000$. Hence $X=683$.

Step 13 (CRT_mod_1000): Compute $n\bmod 1000$.

First modulo $8$: $29\equiv 5\pmod{8}$ and $5^2\equiv 1\pmod{8}$, so $29^{\text{even}}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$ and $29^{\text{odd}}\equiv 5\pmod{8}$. Therefore
$$
n=1+29^6+29^{15}+29^{23}+29^{31}\equiv 1+1+5+5+5=17\equiv 1\pmod{8}.
$$

Next modulo $125$: we use the computed residues
$$
29^5\equiv 24\pmod{125},\quad 29^6\equiv 24\cdot 29=696\equiv 71\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^{10}\equiv 24^2\equiv 76\pmod{125},\quad 29^{15}\equiv 29^{10}\cdot 29^5\equiv 76\cdot 24=1824\equiv 74\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^{20}\equiv 76^2\equiv 26\pmod{125},\quad 29^3\equiv 29^2\cdot 29\equiv 91\cdot 29=2639\equiv 14\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^{23}\equiv 29^{20}\cdot 29^3\equiv 26\cdot 14=364\equiv 114\pmod{125},
$$
$$
29^{25}\equiv -1\pmod{125},\quad 29^{31}\equiv 29^{25}\cdot 29^6\equiv -71\equiv 54\pmod{125}.
$$
Hence
$$
n\equiv 1+71+74+114+54=314\equiv 64\pmod{125}.
$$

Now solve
$$
n\equiv 1\pmod{8},\qquad n\equiv 64\pmod{125}.
$$
Write $n\equiv 64+125t$. Modulo $8$ this is $n\equiv 0+5t\pmod{8}$, so we need $5t\equiv 1\pmod{8}$, giving $t\equiv 5\pmod{8}$. Taking $t=5$ gives
$$
n\equiv 64+625=689\pmod{1000}.
$$
So $n\bmod 1000=689$.

Step 14 (modular_reduction_mod_1000): Evaluate the defining congruence for $N$ modulo $1000$.

First reduce the needed powers of $p$ modulo $1000$:
$$
p^2=29^2=841,
$$
$$
p^3=29^3=24389\equiv 389\pmod{1000},
$$
$$
p^4=29^4=707281\equiv 281\pmod{1000}.
$$
Thus
$$
(p^4+3)\equiv 284,\quad (p^2+7)\equiv 848,\quad (p+11)=40,\quad (p^3+5)\equiv 394\pmod{1000}.
$$

Using $C=22$, $W=371$, $V=22$, $Q=8115$, $X=683$, and $n\equiv 689\pmod{1000}$, compute each term modulo $1000$:
$$
(p^4+3)C\equiv 284\cdot 22=6248\equiv 248,
$$
$$
(p^2+7)W\equiv 848\cdot 371=314608\equiv 608,
$$
$$
(p+11)V\equiv 40\cdot 22=880,
$$
$$
(p^3+5)Q\equiv 394\cdot 8115\equiv 394\cdot 115=45310\equiv 310\pmod{1000}.
$$
Now sum:
$$
248+608+880+310+683+689=3418\equiv 418\pmod{1000}.
$$
So $N\equiv 418\pmod{1000}$.

Step 15 (normalization): The unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv 418\pmod{1000}$ is
$$
\boxed{418}.
$$

3) Uniqueness Test

(1) The integers $n$ and $k$ are uniquely determined by their definitions.

(2) By Lemma 1, each difference $p^t-p^s$ expands as a fixed block of base-$p$ digits equal to $p-1$ on the interval $[s,t-1]$. In this instance, the four intervals are disjoint, so the base-$p$ digit expansion of $n-k$ is uniquely forced.

(3) With the base-$p$ digits of $k$ and $n-k$ fixed, the digit-by-digit addition algorithm deterministically produces the carry sequence $(c_i)$. Lemma 2 shows that each $(p-1)$ block forces carries $c_i=1$ throughout the block and forces the carry to stop immediately afterward (because the next digits are both $0$). Hence the carry set, and therefore $C$, $W$, and $Q$, are uniquely determined.

(4) By Kummer's theorem (Lemma 3), $V=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries, so $V$ is uniquely determined.

(5) The residue $A\equiv p^W+5C\pmod{125}$ is uniquely determined. The congruence $y^2\equiv A\pmod{125}$ has exactly two solutions because it has exactly two solutions modulo $5$ and each lifts uniquely to modulo $125$ by Lemma 5. The two lifted roots sum to $125$, so they have opposite parity; the additional invariant $y\equiv C\pmod{2}$ selects exactly one, making $y$ unique.

(6) Since $\gcd(125,8)=1$, CRT gives a unique residue class modulo $1000$ for the system $X\equiv y\pmod{125}$ and $X\equiv Q\pmod{8}$. The constraint $0\le X<1000$ selects a unique integer representative.

(7) Finally, the defining congruence for $N$ is a single residue class modulo $1000$, and $0\le N<1000$ selects a unique integer. Therefore the computed answer $N=418$ is unique.

418
